evals.prompt: My feature branch has five commits and three of them are just 'wip', 'more wip', 'fix'. I haven't pushed yet. Clean up the history into a couple of sensible commits before I open a PR.
evals.expected_output: An interactive rebase (or a scripted non-interactive equivalent) that squashes the WIP commits into meaningful ones and rewords messages, done only because the branch is unpushed, with no force-push needed to a shared branch.
assertions: Uses git rebase (interactive or a scripted GIT_SEQUENCE_EDITOR/autosquash equivalent) to squash the WIP commits
Confirms or relies on the branch being unpushed/unshared before rewriting history
Does not use git revert to clean up local unshared commits
Final history has fewer, meaningfully-named commits rather than the WIP chain

evals.prompt: I just ran git reset --hard HEAD~3 on my branch and realized I actually needed those three commits. They're gone from git log. Can I get them back?
evals.expected_output: Recovery via git reflog: find the pre-reset HEAD entry and git reset --hard back to it (or cherry-pick the commit shas), with the explanation that committed work is reachable from the reflog and was not truly lost.
assertions: Directs the user to git reflog to find the commit before the reset
Recovers by resetting (or branching/cherry-picking) back to the reflog sha
Explains that committed work is recoverable from the reflog, not truly lost
Does not claim the commits are unrecoverable

evals.prompt: A commit I pushed to our shared main branch last week introduced a bug. I want to undo it. Should I reset main back past it and force-push?
evals.expected_output: Refuse the reset/force-push on shared main; use git revert to add an inverse commit instead, because others have already pulled the history.
assertions: Recommends git revert instead of reset plus force-push
Explains that main is shared and rewriting it breaks everyone who pulled it
Does not suggest force-pushing a rewritten main
Mentions revert creates a new inverse commit that leaves history intact

evals.prompt: I'm four commits deep on my branch and just noticed the second commit is missing a null check. I want that fix to be part of that commit, not a new one on top. How do I do that?
evals.expected_output: The fixup + autosquash workflow: git commit --fixup <sha> for the second commit, then git rebase --autosquash, followed by --force-with-lease if the branch was already pushed.
assertions: Uses git commit --fixup targeting the second commit's sha
Runs git rebase with --autosquash to fold the fixup in
If a force-push is mentioned, it is --force-with-lease rather than --force
Does not just add a separate 'fix null check' commit on top
